content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. If you haven't seen Children of Men (2006), you're missing out on a masterpiece.

**Score:** 254
**Body:** I rewatched this recently and it absolutely blew me away all over again. It's one of those movies that just gets better every time. It's a bleak story, for sure, but it's also incredibly gripping and surprisingly hopeful.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Good lord, is this movie almost 20 years old?!
  - I know! It's wild how time flies. But that's the sign of a true classic, it feels just as powerful and relevant today as it did back then.
2. Michael Caine was my favorite.
  - pull my finger
3. Also rewatched it this weekend. I agree. It’s a masterpiece and it improves with every viewing. The cinematography alone 🤯
4. Ooh this is ripe for a rewatch, thanks for the reminder! I'm less grateful for how old you've made me feel though lol, I can't believe its been 20 odd years since this came out!
5. It's my favourite movie.
6. my favorite dystopian film.
7. This is a top 5 movie of all time for me , I recommend this all the time I can’t tell enough people about it
8. Pull mah fingah

## 2. Looking for movies where the main character is a musician

**Score:** 39
**Body:** I want to find films where the protagonist is a musician. It should not just be background music but their life as a performer or artist should be central to the plot.

Can anyone recommend good movies focused on musicians? I am open to any genre including biopics, dramas, or comedies. Thank you for your suggestions.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Amadeus 

Inside Llewyn Davis

Tar

A Mighty Wind
2. Walk Hard
3. Purple Rain
4. The Commitments (1991)
5. The Pianist
6. Mr Holland’s Opus 

Whiplash 

The Sound of Metal
7. School of Rock

Soul
8. Whiplash
9. Hedwig and the Angry Inch
10. Shine (1996)

## 3. Suggest me a movie where Robert De Niro isn't just playing "Robert De Niro"

**Score:** 30
**Body:** You know what I mean. I love him, but sometimes it feels like he's just playing a variation of the same intense, tough guy in a suit. I want to see a film where he truly disappears into a role that doesn't rely on that familiar persona.

What's a performance where he genuinely surprises you by not being the typical De Niro character? I'm looking for something where he shows a different side of his range.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jackie Brown.

He's literally the anti-de niro, total shlub.
  - “When you robbed banks, did you have to look for your car then too?”

“Is it this aisle or the next one over?”

“Louisssss.”
2. Awakenings
  - +1 to Awakenings! This movie made me see him as an actor in a whole different light. Plus it’s just a great, heartbreaking story.
3. Stardust - plays a gay pirate
4. The King of Comedy
5. Brazil
6. The Intern
7. Mary Shelley's Frankenstein (1994)
8. It’s hilarious how many different answers there are here…

## 4. Melancholy in European cities

**Score:** 27
**Body:** On the lookout for films set in Europe with vibe similar to:

- One Fine Morning (2022)
- The Worst Person in the World (2021)
- Call Me by Your Name (2017)
- Certified Copy (2010)
- The Before Trilogy
- The Three Colors Trilogy (1993-94)
- Dekalog Series (1989)
- Alice in the Cities (1974)
- Masculin Feminin (1966)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. like [In Bruges](https://www.themoviedb.org/movie/8321-in-bruges) (2008)?
2. Submarine (2010)
3. Wings of desire/Der himmel über Berlin
4. Look up the italian masterpiece movie (imo): La grande bellezza. It's set in Rome, Italy.
5. Oslo, August 31st (2011)

The Fire Within (1963)

both based on the novel Le fou follet by Pierre Drieu La Rochelle
6. fallen leaves (2023)
7. Once (2007)
8. The single most melancholic film I have ever seen is The Hairdressers Husband (Le Mari de la coiffeuse), FR 1990. It only has some elements of coming of age and is mostly focused on MC as an adult.

For a European coming of age film, it’d be hard to beat Cinema Paradiso, ITA 1988, though.
9. The Beat That My Heart Skipped (2005) could fit that description
10. if you liked KIeślowski, you might enjoy plenty of old polish cinema. Try Innocent sorcerers , How to be loved or Salto

## 5. Easy watch, funny.

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Hi all! 
I’m recovering from surgery and have no emotional capacity for anything and yet tons of time to kill st home. I want to watch films that are easy and fun/ebtertaining with substance. Old or new. Any genre. 
Thanks a million.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What about Bob
2. Saved! 

It’s a sarcastic comedy about a girl at private school who becomes pregnant by her gay boyfriend. It’s as ridiculous as it sounds. It really makes fun of the whole private Christian school stereotype but has a sweet message about making choices that are right for you instead of being a follower. 


Best In Show is super easy to watch when your brain is lazy. 


Miss Congeniality 


George of the Jungle


Hamlet 2


I also just watched My Date With The Presidents Daughter for the first time in like 15 years and it is soooo good
3. Raising Arizona  
The Big Lebowski  
The Blues Brothers
  - Raising Arizona is very funny. 😆
4. Living In Oblivion (1995). 

Delightful character comedy on the set of a low-budget movie. Dream-like. Lots of references which it doesn't matter if you get or don't. An all time favourite of mine.
5. Roxanne (Steve Martin, Daryl Hannah)

All of Me (Steve Martin, Lily Tomlin)

(Came back to add):

Trading Places (Eddie Murphy, Dan Aykroyd)

A Fish Called Wanda (John Cleese, Jamie Lee Curtis; Kevin Kline won an Oscar for his hilarious role!)
6. Cool Runnings (1993)
7. Almost Famous is one of my comfort films
8. Give the original UK version of Death At a Funeral a watch.

If you like horror, you cannot go wrong with Tucker and Dale vs Evil
9. The Fundamentals of Caring

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What movie is just one big advertisement?

**Score:** 1378
**Body:** Still love this film, but it is just one big ad for Google. Transformers was also another pick.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. War of the worlds starring the Amazon shopping cart
2. The climax of this film shows this scrappy team winning the competition to gain the internship. And the room erupts in applause. Almost like everyone forgot that they were each and every one of them also competing for that same internship and now they are unemployed.

Edit: a word
  - Not that I dislike him, infact quite the opposite but isn’t that the premise of like every Vince Vaughn led comedy? Or even movie? He’s a severely underqualified underdog who everyone loves and cheers for and always ends up winning lol. I’ve always found it funny how he’s constantly pitted as this average Joe (haha) in all his films yet, in reality he’d be an extremely popular, rooted for and celebrated kinda guy.
3. The Lego movie
  - The best ad ever made
    - EVERYTHING IS AWESOME!
4. The fast and the furious is a big Corona ad in some parts.
5. Happy Gilmore 2
6. Evolution
  - “Haven’t you noticed how shiny and flake-free our hair is?”

## 2. Movies with an opening scene that is vastly superior to the rest of the film?

**Score:** 682

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. X-men Origins: Wolverine
  - Just watch the opening, then jump right to The Wolverine. Or better yet, watch the opening then read a few comics. It's the best way.
2. 28 weeks later
3. Up  
  
And it's not that the rest of the movie is bad.
  - YES! I’m in awe of how Pixar created an entire love story starting from kids meeting to old age and one passing away, with just a few words and then a montage… if that was a “Pixar Short”, I’d call it a masterpiece. 

As it is, it’s a masterpiece opening of a very ***SQUIRREL*** good movie.
4. The new mortal kombat. It started out very cool in feudal Japan and then turned into the weirdest modern MMA tournament battle with very bad acting and wardrobes.
5. The first Judge Dredd
6. 28 weeks later. Not that the rest of the movie is terrible, it is fine at worst and good at best. But that opening scene is fucking amazing
7. Gangs of New York, the whole movie is amazing but that opening scene with Liam neeson is another level
8. Valerian and the City of a Thousand Planets


Epic opening montage, trash movie. 

## 3. I was kind of blown away by Kurt Russell's performance in "The Hateful 8"

**Score:** 508
**Body:** I mean, the guy is one of the best actors Hollywood has ever produced, so I shouldn't be too surprised. But his character was such a far departure from his usual roles I thought. In a movie that had some of the best (or my favorite) performances I have ever seen, (Walton Goggins should have won an award for his role in my opinion, he was so good in this movie), and Samual L Jackson of course played his character phenomenally well, as he pretty much does in every single role he plays, but Kurt Russell was absolutely hilarious in this movie. And one tough dude. He just got outsmarted and drank poisoned coffee unfortunately. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. (Spoilers) My favorite part was when Chris Mannix [Walton Goggins] tells John "the Hangman" Ruth about the letter being fake. That whole interaction between those two actors and Samuel L. Jackson in that one scene is pure gold.
2. JJL stole the show for me. I never thought much of her as an actress but that one really blew me away
  - Yes, she nailed that role.
  - Soo unlikeable and creepy.
3. One of my favorite scenes in The Hateful Eight is when Kurt Russell’s character finds out that Samuel L. Jackson's character lied about the Lincoln letter. It’s funny and revealing — this tough, violent, prideful man gets genuinely hurt over something as simple as being lied to. The contrast between his brutal exterior and his emotional reaction makes the scene unexpectedly human and darkly humorous.
  - It’s such a good scene. I think that scene is why Samuel L makes sure to hang JJL in the end. 

John Ruth was all about doing things the right way, all the time every time. His people always hang. 

Whereas Samuel L is a survivalist. He will do whatever it takes at any time in order to survive. So to him lying is nothing. It’s just a means to an end. Same as burning the encampment. 

The whole idea of that is anathema to how John Ruth lives his life. It simply never entered his head that Samuel L would lie. 

It’s such a great scene and hammers home the difference in these two bounty hunters.
4. Check out Bone Tomahawk also, if you haven't.
5. Kurt has been making movies since he was practically a baby … he’s got tons of experience. To me if he’s in a movie it’s going to decent if not great. He’s made some bangers for sure !
6. so was i, said the guitare.
7. daisy say hi to major warren

## 4. Which actor/actress gives the most consistently great performances?

**Score:** 451
**Body:** At long last, the time has come. It is time to preach the Gospel of Gosling.

Ryan Gosling’s streak began with Half Nelson in 2006, and over the next decade and a half, he has assembled an enviable and eclectic filmography, but one thing has remained consistent – the strength of his performances.

Lars and the Real Girl and Fracture were serviceable aperitifs, but it was with the beginning of the next decade that Gosling the performer truly came alive.

In my opinion, he’s been one of the finest actors of the past decade, and I’ll tell you why.

His current streak began in 2010 with Derek Cianfrance’s achingly heartfelt romantic drama Blue Valentine.

Then came The Ides of March, where he went toe-to-toe with George Clooney. This was around the time I had begun to ‘clock’ him, so to speak.

“Huh, so this Gosling guy is pretty good.”

His contrasting roles in Drive and Crazy Stupid Love only increased my admiration. And by the time the criminally underseen The Place Beyond The Pines came out, I was sold; hook, line and sinker.

“Okay, he’s quite something.”

I’ve been an admirer ever since. Gosling has established a bit of a reputation for playing stoic and slightly aloof characters. But what fascinates me is the variety that he brings to such parts.

I find it slightly difficult to articulate. There are different pitches to his stoicism; he modulates it according to the character. It’s subtle, but it’s there. And I find that highly impressive.

On the surface level, his characters in Drive, The Place Beyond the Pines and Blade Runner 2049 are quite similar. Each a variation on the strong, silent and badass type.

But his silences convey different meanings in all three movies.

Through vacant glances and precise expressions that barely register, Gosling creates three distinct, tangible and wholly believable characters; essentially working off the same template.

Another engrossing chapter of the Gospel of Gosling arrived in 2016 with The Nice Guys where Gosling turns in one of my favourite comedic performances of the 21st Century.

His seamless transition from a stoic, silent badass to a motormouth, goofy dumbass is a testament to his skill as an actor.

And at this point, I was convinced he’s a bit special. I can’t think of many current actors, if any, who are so exceptionally proficient at both comedy and drama.

“Right, this guy is exceptional.”

Robert Downey Jr’s performance in Tropic Thunder is one of my favourite comedic performances of all time. But for all his ability, I’ve yet to be bowled over by him in a dramatic performance.

Gosling does it time and time again. In Damien Chazelle’s somewhat overlooked First Man, the actor ventured once more into the ‘stoic’ well.

But his interpretation of Neil Armstrong is layered with a depth of emotion. And Gosling conveys those emotions with astonishing economy.

Ultimately, Gosling is proficient at the one trait that leaves me in awe of certain actors – doing a lot with very little.

He’s always real; he’s always believable – whether he’s a Jazz musician (La La Land), an astronaut or even an android. He embodies his characters with a degree of authenticity that elevates every film he’s a part of.

All things considered, I think he has been one of the best actors of the past decade, and he’s got the filmography to back it up.

In terms of consistency, Gosling is right up there with my two other favourites of the past decade, Jake Gyllenhaal and Michael Fassbender.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sam Rockwell
  - I can't see anybody else taking the over-the-top comic relief role in Galaxy Quest and elevating it to the status he did. Just a small footnote in his career but an example of him making a meal out of very little
2. Philip Seymour Hoffman.

Imo the most consistent actor ever.
3. Gary Oldman
4. The best actor I’ve met is the dude at my job that acts like he is sick or don’t feel good to leave work earlier
5. Matthew Lillard,my man got some SERIOUS range.Goes from lovable goofball to maniacal killer instantly
6. Jake Gyllenhaal and Christian Bale
7. My favorite actor is Robert Duvall, everything I’ve seen him in he’s absolute gold.
8. Leo
9. Paul Dano

## 5. The Running Man - Cheesy and campy as hell, but also highly entertaining.  Also, tons of classic one-liners from Arnold.  "Hey, Killian! Here's Subzero! Now... plain zero!"

**Score:** 307

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Also an ever relevant message about controlling the public through media propaganda.
  - [Who Loves You](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zAEpxIpu-wY)
2. I'll tell you what I think of it! I live to see you eat that contract, but I hope you leave enough room for my fist because I'm going to ram it into your stomach and break your goddamn spine!
3. Tons of one-liners from Arnold, for sure, but the best one liner comes from Richard Dawson's evil Damon Killian in response to what was to that point Arnold's across movies one liner (you know the one!):

"Only in reruns."
4. He had to split
5. One of the most entertaining movies based on a book by Stephen King.

I would still love to see a book accurate adaptation though.
  - Supposedly, the version coming out in November will be closer to the book.
6. I love that it takes place in 2018.
7. Dynamo is the weirdest character in any 80’s movie. That includes Gremlins 2, Big Trouble in Little China AND Roadhouse. I’ll die on this hill.
8. Arnold: "I'll be back."

Killian: "Only in a re-run."

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Just watched Paris, Texas for the first time

**Score:** 78
**Body:** Man, what a movie. Haven’t seen something which has given me so many thoughts in a long time, so I will post my interpretation and I invite others to post theirs.

Firstly, I was immensely shocked and honestly a little revolted by the revelation behind Travis’s disappearance. Throughout the whole movie I wasn’t quite sure about him, on the one hand he seemed to have some compassion, shining the shoes of his family, having a desire to bond and connect with the song he left behind. On the other hand, I felt he was quite a selfish character, that he didn’t quite respect the kindness his brother and his wife were showing him by picking him up and inviting him into their home. He reminded me of some addicts I knew, people who we tried to help and who we thought were good people at heart, but were ultimately unable to reciprocate the hospitality and effort we put into helping them out.

We of course eventually found out that he was the master of his own destiny. He abused his wife, who was much younger than him, and the burning of their trailer was really his fault. He ran away because he was traumatized but also because he couldn’t face the consequences of his own actions, which in turn had a negative effect on the other people who cared about him. Really, Travis has a negative impact on basically everyone in his life, he does develop a relationship with his son but also uproots him from his comfortable life and puts him in quite an unfair situation. 

The great ambiguity of the movie then is if we are supposed to feel any sympathy for him at all. He’s not a sociopath, but he also *still* does not understand how his actions affect other people, he still gets jealous of his wife after all that has happened, and he still abandons his son without even giving him a proper goodbye. He is a truly pathetic character.

In a contemporary context, I think this movie is especially powerful and shocking. I think that ‘cancel culture’ so to speak dehumanizes people who commit such acts as Travis, and I think a lot of people are actually happy with that. This movie on the other hand dares to humanize a character who *morally* is really quite unforgivable. It presents a question of how exactly morality is expressed in cinema, how a movie about a straight white male character who is essentially the enemy of leftist social politics would not fit in to this our current climate. Why should the movie focus on him and not Jane or his son? 

I’m not quite sure about how I feel about all of this yet, so I would love to hear some other opinions on what this movie represents.



### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It’s a very difficult movie for sure

The complete abandonment of his brother’s family in the second act threw me for a loop but it is very much on point for what Travis does. Completely abandons those who cared for him once his focus is on something else

Incredible performances by Stanton and Stockwell and the cinematography is sublime.

I absolutely hated Texas when I was there but it can really set the scene for bleakness as evidenced by Hud and The Last Picture Show
  - Why did you hate Texas?
2. I think a lot of your take is fair, I read it a bit differently though. I do think the movie is calling for us to sympathize with Travis, but not necessarily to forgive him. His final act at the end seemed symbolic to me of the idea that what he had done in the past couldn't be forgiven, so there was no way for him to stay and make things work with his family. At the same time, there was an element of redemption in that he brought together two people who he saw as pure and still able to have love. Despite all of his selfish acts, this was one unselfish act that brought about good--even if he could never undo and perhaps should never be forgiven for his past misdeeds.
3. I also just watched this movie for the first time and was profoundly impacted by it. I will start by saying the analysis of this film has probably shifted dramatically from when it was released to now, and it's hard not to look at it from a 1984 perspective. I think Travis can best be described as being the embodiment of guilt and shame for his actions. He takes this so far that he has shunned himself to be a wanderer in the wilderness, and (convienantly) forgot who he is. He was so used to being a wanderer that it was hard for him to be in houses like with his brithers family, and he exhibited OCD like behaviors  (shoes on the wall) as a coping mechanism. I will say that in 1984 or 2025, Travis' shame is still all our shared shame, and it touches on the fragility of masculinity as well as the conundrum of jealousy and possesivness He knows how bad he messed up his chance for a true love relationship, and he knows he can never go back to the one he lost; for fear that his negative emotional behaviors will return. You know, so much of this movie is left to interpretation that I like to think there was a chance that he could  have reconciled with Jane, but that might be 2025 talking and not 1984. Great film, watch it if you love chatachter driven films with great music and cinematography.
4. well you've hit upon the exact tension that most public discourse never really addresses. there are individual stories of people who are genuinely suffering and cause genuine suffering and then there are groups of people we can identify in several ways (gender, race, religion, etc.) that we generalize about and judge harshly because at least we're not like THEM. We've dehumanized each other into stereotypes instead of acknowledging each other as complex human beings and we're watching what that dehumanization turns into in real time in Chicago right now.

it seems like a lot of people watch TV shows and movies in order to sit in judgment of the characters' words and deeds. Travis tries to feel the true depth of the repercussions of what he did and put something right after causing so much pain, which is all one can really do. it's deeply felt and deeply thought through just like Wenders' other work. I feel much more connected to humanity after watching movies from him, Jean Renoir, Lubitsch, Ozu.
5. I think the film is simply about change, not in any grandiose manner, but in ways that are subtle and meaningful to those who have been hurt. I don't think Travis is supposed to come across as pathetic, rather as someone who's come to his own terms with the hurt he's sown and truly found forgiveness in others in the only way he knows how (leaving them be but mending their own connections). The resolution of the film comes with the resolution of his relationship in limbo, after everyone was consciously or unconsciously waiting for him to make that final move. I think this has a lot to say about how forgiveness should be measured by individuals
6. I think the most important part of that movie is with Travis telling and then retelling of the story regarding his Mother. When we first hear him tell his brother the story it’s a jovial childhood memory. He clearly loves his Father otherwise he wouldn’t have gotten the land in Paris. 

But then we see Travis tell his son the same story but with an entirely different tone. The son sat in a chair (like a therapist) while Travis lays on a couch spilling his heart out. And admitting that his own Father wasn’t the good guy after all. And maybe HE, Travis,  isn’t the good guy after all. 

It’s at that moment that I believe he decides he is giving his kid up to his mom.. Travis married his too young wife because he wanted a “fancy” woman. Someone special. In that moment he realizes he is just like his own dad
7. This is a recurring theme in Wenders.

It starts with the Goalie's Anxiety at the Penalty Kick.  We see him commit a horrible act and go on the run. We obviously don't like him, but because the story is told through him we can feel his anxiety as he waits to be caught. It's a human emotion we all have even if we haven't done something as horrible.

In Perfect Day we don't know what he did to make him withdraw from life and we do sympathize with him. But we should be open to the possibility that he did something horrible, especially since it is Wenders. I at least didn't let that possibility change my feelings for the character.

You can make similar analysis of Kings of the Road and Alice in the Cities, which is about an abandoned child and I don't judge the mom in it that harshly.

We've all done bad things and hurt people. Hopefully not as bad as these, but I think it is very human to think about these regrets and pain.
8. I’m kind of biased because I’m a father to a young kid and I grew up never having a relationship with my own biological father (who is a selfish and immature asshole) so I’ve always struggled with my feelings about this movie. I don’t like Travis very much. He’s a tragic figure for sure, but I often see people position him as a tragic hero who makes a noble decision to reunite his son with his mother. I don’t think there’s anything noble in what he does. What he does is highly disruptive and selfish. The noble thing would’ve been leaving his son in the care of his brother and sister-in-law. It’s fine that this doesn’t happen, the film tells the story it wants to tell, and I don’t have an aversion to movies about flawed, unlikable protagonists, but I found the romantic, bittersweet tone of it to be kind of off putting, especially because he’s never really held accountable by anyone. I don’t see forgiveness in this movie, I see a lack of accountability.

I need to rewatch it to see if my feelings have changed at all, but that’s how I’ve always felt about it.

## 2. Just watched Moonlight (2016) — what a masterpiece

**Score:** 62
**Body:** Just watched Moonlight (2016), and wow — one of the greatest movies I’ve ever seen. The cinematography and screenplay are just stunning.

(Spoiler Alert 🚨)

It’s about the life of a black man — his struggles at school, at home, with his friend, with his lover. He’s gay, and his whole life feels like a fight to escape pain. He meets a man who, ironically, leads him toward drugs as a kid, and that becomes part of his escape.

In the end, he meets his mom, meets his lover — and it’s just perfect. A beautiful look at the struggles of black men and the black community.

Also, can someone explain what “BLACK 305” on his car plate meant?

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Agree. I found a copy of the screenplay in a book shop recently; just reading the last scene gave me goose bumps. 

Black is the main character’s nickname. 305 is the area code for Miami
  - The last scene is so beautiful. 


Also, the diner scene, dear Lord. 🥲
  - Thank you very much for the explanation brother 👍🏾♥️
  - That’s a good call to read the screen play!! Love it
2. I remember seeing it in theaters with an ex girlfriend. We knew the bare minimum about it. I walked out completely gobsmacked. We walked across the street to have drinks afterwards and could stop talking about it. We spent the next week reading anything written about it. I was so stoked when it won best picture (fiasco notwithstanding). Truly a modern day masterpiece, and I don't say that lightly because I fear that word is becoming too trite.
3. In the end, when you realize that was the first and last time he’d ever been with anyone… it just hits you. Like a punch to the stomach. It was the saddest I've ever felt at the end of a movie.

## 3. Can we find meaning in David Lynch’s Inland Empire by looking at it through the lens of Simulacra and Simulation by Jean Baudrillard?

**Score:** 23
**Body:** This is hard because im not really sure i fully understand either of these things. Im thinking about when nikki says “damn, this sounds like a dialogue from our script”  she can’t differentiate the real from the simulation. And the first movie that was made, that nikki is remaking, was never finished, so that would be simulacrum Right? A copy without an original. It seems like throughout the whole movie everything progresses the same way Baudrillard described the 4 stages of simulacra, getting more and more out of touch with reality and eventually there is no identifiable reality. The “lost girl” watching the tv in some scenes experiences her life through cinematic images, mirroring us watching her. Which highlights Hollywood as hyperreality, simulations feel more real than the real. Baudrillard claims that in the world of simulacra, meaning implodes because signs no longer refer to anything solid. They just circulate endlessly. And Similarly Inland Empire refuses narrative clarity or closure. Its confusing structure, nonlinear editing, and repetition mimic the endless feedback loops of simulation. But also idk I guess a good question would also be can we Better understand Simulacra and Simulation By Looking at it through the lens of inland empire. But I don’t know guys it’s 6am and I haven’t slept let me know if anyone is picking up what I’m putting down and can add anything or tell me I’m wrong and crazy and not understanding 

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I wasn't sold from the post title but I think you back up your argument pretty well. The unfinished nature of the original movie is a kind of cloud hanging over everything, almost as much as the fact that the production seems to have been cursed. I hadn't noticed that, not consciously.

When Theroux says he doesn't do remakes it's almost a humorous line, because the original movie was never finished, we never see one second of footage from it or so much as a poster or photo of an actor. So what exactly is being remade? We're just told this original exists and has been successfully repressed by the studio, but for all intents and purposes it doesn't really exist. But for the rest of the movie when we see an unexplained character or situation we're left wondering if it's some aspect of the original cursed production, the original folk tale it was based on, or something else that overlaps with these (like the "old tale" the woman neighbor tells Nikki at the beginning).

A lesser movie (or maybe one made more consciously to close its circles) probably would have told us more about the original cursed production. But having it be vague and for all purposes non-existent makes it so much more haunting to the narrative. It's more like a movie whose title and lead actor and reputation you know, without knowing anything else, for which you've imagined for years what it might be about and even conjured images in your head based on nothing whatsoever.
  - >It's more like a movie whose title and lead actor and reputation you know, without knowing anything else, for which you've imagined for years what it might be about and even conjured images in your head based on nothing whatsoever.

This is pretty much 90% of online film discourse these days...

But yeah, never seeing the actual "original" or "source" of the mutated facsimiles is very much an aspect of the simulacra. Corrupted information shorn of context and deliberately mutated to create a recontextualised reality. 

I imagine the now much-maligned Marvel movies were looking to bring some of Baudrlilard's ideas more mainstream (like The Matrix beforehand) with their Multiverse arc, and somewhat ironically bend them to fit their own intentions, but the Overton Window shifted.

Which leads me to considering what inaugurated that shift. There's more than one reason obviously, but so far the anointed suspects don't really satisfy me...
    - Just want to chip in that another great movie to look from a Baudrilliardian lens would be Synechdoce, New York!

(Simulation not being divided into separate worlds, like ideal, material, mimesis etc. but as something happening in this world, eventually replacing the real (or something))
          - I'm glad you caught my little joke there!
      - Good shout. 
Someone smarter and more knowledgeable than me should make a list of Baudrlilard-influenced flicks.
Last Year at Marienbaad and Solaris fit for me but predate his book by 20 years and a decade respectively. Could make a case for Vertigo as the OG simulacra movie too.

Maybe Dark City. Possibly Pulp Fiction. Her, Adaptation and Inception squeeze in too.
      - Putting that up on my "to watch" whiteboard, thanks. I love this kind of story.
2. I'm not sure there's anything in 2025 that you can't smash Baudrillard into & have something meaningful fall out—I had a professor who put that up against the Josie & the Pussycats movie, to surprising effect lol.
3. I'm too much of a naive realist to have much to add, but I imagine Baudrillard himself would've approved of your interpretation. In an [interview](https://jcgaal.medium.com/the-matrix-decoded-le-nouvel-observateur-interview-with-jean-baudrillard-cc8b293cd499) expressing his frustration with *The Matrix*, he singled out *Mulholland Drive* as a "masterpiece" and one of the films that explored his ideas well. 

If there's any connective tissue between Lynch's worldview and Baudrillard's, it's probably the Vedic concept of [maya](https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maya_(religion)), or immanent reality as a divine illusion. I’d be curious to know whether Baudrillard ever commented on maya, even if it’s quite remote from the Situationist framework his ideas first evolved in.

## 4. Why are some films so mediocre even though they have all the same ingredients as great movies?

**Score:** 6
**Body:** Hi everyone,

I watched "Play Dirty" on Amazon Prime today. It's a movie directed by Shane Black. Now, Kiss Kiss Bang Bang and Nice Guys are two of my favorite movies. And he wrote Lethal Weapon which I also consider to be a great movie (ignore the future Mel Gibson, it was 1987).

Play Dirty had all the ingredients of previous Shane Black movies that I liked. It had the funny (in intention) dialogue. Goofy bad dudes. It was even sort of set in Christmas, I think it was mentioned in passing. The part where two bad guys had Mark Walberg cornered was really similar to the part where the bad guys had RDJ cornered in KKBB. And a lot more.

Yet the movie felt very boring. A real slog to watch though to the end. The characters, while it felt like actors were doing good job, (I mean I love Keegan Michael Key), they were kinda phoning it in. I don't know exactly how to pinpoint why it felt like such a mediocre movie.

I recently felt like this while watching "The Killer" by David Fincher on Netflix. It was also not a bad movie but it just felt so, I don't know mundane or something. Like why is such a talented director who has made such great movies in the past spending his time doing something 50 other directors can do just as well?

 Why do you all think that is? We as the audience seem to be able to sniff out when a director don't seem to be at the top of their game anymore but I'm sure they don't do it intentionally.



### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It just proves that film making is alchemy, at least in terms of feature length narratives. There's simply too many people and pieces involved to reduce it down to a formula for success.

It was made just two years after Halloween, with a lot of the same cast & crew, and while I wouldn't call it bad (I enjoyed watching it) it didn't really amount to much more than a cozy campfire story. Halloween has an even more minimalist story yet it helped create its own subgenre, and 50yrs on is still pretty harrowing to sit through. Who could have predicted that?

7. Maybe your tastes are diverging. For the sake of discussion this could be in a few ways:


- Maybe you actually want more than justa fun story. A good script doesnt guarantee a good final project. Shane Black vehicles are consistent in the“two-hander feat. goofy characters and snappy irony-tinged action set pieces” formula. But maybe this isnt enough for you. Perhaps you enjoyed KKBB and Nice Guys because both films featured very talented actors who brought shine to the formula. Agree to disagree about LW1: i think mel gibson’s whole ptsd tasmanian devil characterization of Riggs is unwatchably cartoonish. Imo Joe Pesci helped balance some of that in the other non-SB penned movies. 


- similarly, maybe your tastes are just diverging naturally. My adolescence was spent watching Alien 3, The Game, Se7en, and Fight Club. Fincher used to be synonymous with grit and unravelling and unease. Starting with Panic Room things started to get more slick, more emotional distance from characters emotions, more plots dominated by (wo)man with a sophisticated plan succeeding step by step. I don’t mind watching movies where people are defined by their job/obsession; i just want to also see the personal & emotional cost of that obsession (ex, The Post, Heat, A Most Violent Year). To me, Fincher films stopped feeling like they captured a part of the human experience that gets overshadowed and instead felt like advanced cinematography exercises with big name actors playing unlikeable or unrelatable characters solving complex problems. When i was younger i definitely labeled that as him losing his touch. But actually its reverso, i expected things from his films that weren’t the core of his vision for his films and didnt get brought along as he became more powerful and autonomous from the studio notes. 

(Actually this is why I personally enjoyed the killer in part because it dropped all pretense at character dramatic arc, was so meticulously and icily composed and under 2 hours, i felt like i was watching an aquarium screensaver, cept instead of tropical fish blowing tiny bubbles, its a dude in a bucket hat doing lots of yoga and having to kill people)


- but also, and related still, these “mediocre” titles you listed are streaming era films. Even when styles they are color graded so as to be playable across many different platforms - and it feels cold and sterile. I dont want to watch movies that feel like Hallmark movies of the week or Verizon commercials. 

Theres a reason so many people care about film grain in the age of digital. Subtle details about how things look subconsciously impacts how we take in what we watch. 
Personally i cant make it thru many netflix shows because of the way they all share the netflix look. Its already 1 or 2 strikes in my mind. Im already poised to see and even exaggerate the severity of “lazy writing,” poor plotting, bad direction, etc. But then ill go watch some 70s horror movie or a mid Shaw Bros film that has a plot you could fit on a bar napkin and terrible voice acting and im like “ooh la la, im watching a film. Let me withold judgment and take in this work of art as a whole”


Thanks for reading. Just some thoughts.

Edit: spelling & general sloppy grammars
8. I think I'm a pretty decent cook, but sometimes I can still screw up a dish even if I'm following (or more likely riffing on) a very reliable recipe that I've made a hundred times. It might have the same ingredients as great meals I've made before, and I probably used the exact same pots and pans and utensils I used before, but for one reason or another, it just didn't turn out.  
  
I usually don't make a *bad* meal this way, but I have definitely made a mediocre meal out of a recipe that yielded great meals in the past. Maybe the bok choy I got from the farmer's market that week just wasn't the best, maybe I threw something in the wok just a little too late, maybe I did a taste test from an especially spicy spoonful so I ended up underspicing everything else. A lot of little things can go wrong that don't necessarily spoil the whole dish, but just make it a little more bland and a little less special.

Maybe I'm oversimplifying it, but "that's life" feels like the honest answer.

## 5. Films featuring in Hitchcock/Truffaut

**Score:** 7
**Body:** I just bought Hitchcock/Truffaut haven't started reading it yet, I really want to, but I don't wanna get spoiled about any film in this books. What should I do? Watch all the Hitchcock movies before reading it, go watching the films when they appear in the book or just let it happen.

This probably sounds ridiculous, but I don't really enjoy watching a movie knowing whats gonna happen

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. They do talk about what happens in the movies, so if you hate spoilers, better watch the movies first. You can watch a movie and read the section about it.

Apparently I need to add more text. 

You could also make sure you watch the big movies and let the other ones go — you're not missing a ton if you read about *Champagne* or *The Ring*, you're potentially ruining a lot more if you read about *Psycho* before watching the movie. 

Or you could cultivate an attitude of zen acceptance about some level of spoilers not ruining movies for you. They're about more than plot, and Hitchcock is a classic example. It's great to be surprised, but the movies are more than surprises.
2. Serious movie studies usually require multiple viewings. Watch it first, without spoilers, then watch a second time after you research everything. You only need to do that with the big ones like psycho, Rear Window and North by Northwest
3. Watching them as you go through the book (movie, then book) sounds like a good time, except there is a bit of bundling going on and I couldn’t guarantee that it’s free of potential spoilers for later films (e.g. discussing the 20s movies but mentioning a connection to a later one). From your last paragraph it sounds like you want to watch as many of the movies as you can first. The book is fascinating and will be there when you’re ready (and will send you back to the movies).

Also there is a film index (at least in my Faber copy), so you can always check if a movie is discussed in a particular section.
4. Better get on and watch a lot of Hitchcocks. Can’t imagine a better way to spend spooky season honestly 👍

Also I’m going to add to this comment otherwise it gets taken down. So let me just say what a fine morning it is, and go Broncos.
5. One thing you should realize before reading the book is that none of what Hitchcock says is accurately transcribed. All his quotes in English were translated into French and then re-translated back into English, so it is only roughly what Hitchcock actually said, not his exact words.

The actual tapes are on Youtube if you want to listen yo what Hitchcock really said:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Jq51gq4s5r4&list=PLrwUnL23zrPvip0v2HuFysocXdw8Ut_k8

(Though I'm not sure if the tapes are 100% complete.)
  - Wow, thanks
6. Definitely watch the movies first - at least all the major films. Spoilers for minor films shouldn't be a big deal. Still that's a lot of movies to watch. 

list of major films: The Lodger, 1934 Man Who Knew Too Much, 39 Steps, Lady Vanishes, Rebecca, Foreign Correspondent, Suspicion, Saboteur, Shadow of Doubt, Lifeboat, Notorious, Rope, Strangers on a Train, Dial M for Murder, Rear Window, To Catch a Thief, 1956 Man Who Knew Too Much, Vertigo, North by Northwest, Psycho, The Birds, Marnie, Frenzy

Only 23 films! And if you watch them in that order it's even more fun.
